metadata.title: Design Your 3 Madison Agents (15 of 30 pts)
metadata.playlist: Plan It Like a PM — Your Madison PRD
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 2 brief — Plan Your Madison Project Like a Pro

beats.narration_text: E5. Three agents, each with one specific job, and a simple picture of how they talk. That is the architecture section — 15 points, and the place where most students either nail the Madison framework or invent things that are not in it. Before you name a single agent, you need to know which Madison layers and components actually exist. That is what this episode is for.

shot.topic: Episode 5 — Design Your 3 Madison Agents (15 pts)
output: 3 agents: name + specific job tied to the PRD problem
Communication diagram: who sends what to whom
Grounded in real Madison layers and components

beats.narration_text: Madison has five layers. Intelligence handles market analysis and brand-perception insight — its named components are MarketMind Research and the knowledge-graph tracking system. Content creates, optimizes, and distributes material — Brand Voice Personalization is the NLP component that lives here. Research runs survey analysis and surfaces customer insight — it works closely with the Intelligence layer. Experience is the customer-facing layer — AI Concierge Systems are here, handling the customer journey in real time. Performance measures and optimizes — Multi-Armed Bandit optimization, using Thompson sampling specifically, lives in this layer. Sitting across all five is the orchestration layer: cross-project validation, dynamic resource allocation, and continuous learning. The orchestration layer is how your agents communicate — it is not one of the five layers, it is the coordination envelope above them. Two integrations: Bellman, which handles reinforcement learning, and Popper, which handles evidence-based validation and bias detection. These are the real Madison components. Before you name an agent, confirm the component you plan to cite actually exists in the Madison README — the link is in the assignment brief. Do not invent features.

shot.new_visual_element: Madison five-layer architecture diagram — labeled with real components and orchestration layer

props.footnote: Verify every component name in the Madison README before putting it on the Figma board.

beats.narration_text: Now pick your three agents. Each one needs three things: a short name, a specific job in one sentence — not 'help the user' but something like 'pull competitor campaign performance data from three named sources and rank them by share-of-voice weekly' — and the Madison layer it lives on, plus the named component if one applies. The three agents together should address your full PRD problem without duplicating each other's job. That last constraint matters: if two agents are doing essentially the same thing with different names, you do not have three agents, you have one agent with an alias. Here is the prompt.

shot.topic: Design 3 Madison agents for my PRD project
shot.command: My PRD problem: [PASTE YOUR PROBLEM STATEMENT]. The Madison layers available: Intelligence, Content, Research, Experience, Performance. Suggest 3 distinct agents for this project. For each: (1) a short agent name, (2) ONE specific job in one sentence — concrete, not vague, (3) which Madison layer it belongs to and which named component (if any) it uses. Make sure the three agents together address the full PRD problem without duplicating each other's job. Flag any agent that does not map cleanly to a real Madison layer.
shot.runningText: designing agents — grounded in real Madison layers…
output: Agent 1: name · specific one-sentence job · Madison layer + component
Agent 2: name · specific one-sentence job · Madison layer + component
Agent 3: name · specific one-sentence job · Madison layer + component
Flag: any agent not cleanly mapped to a real Madison layer

beats.narration_text: A list of three agent descriptions is not an architecture. An architecture shows what passes between the agents. You need a communication diagram: three boxes, labeled arrows, and a label on each arrow showing what data moves. For example: Agent 1 pulls competitor performance data and sends a ranked list to Agent 2. Agent 2 selects the top variants from that list and sends their IDs to Agent 3. Agent 3 runs a Thompson sampling test on those variant IDs and sends optimization results back to the orchestration layer. The orchestration layer sits above the whole diagram as the coordination envelope — it is what ensures cross-project validation happens and resources get reallocated when one agent falls behind. Keep the diagram beginner-simple: three boxes, labeled arrows, one label per arrow saying what the message is. The excellence move is showing the data schema on one of those arrows — the actual fields and types. That one annotation tells the instructor you have thought about the data, not just the boxes.

shot.new_visual_element: Three-agent communication diagram — boxes, labeled arrows, orchestration layer, one data schema label

arrows.label: ranked competitor list
arrows.schemaAnnotation: { source: string, share_of_voice: number, week: ISO8601 }[]

props.footnote: Schema annotation on one arrow = the excellence move

beats.narration_text: The schema annotation is worth doing correctly. Once you know which two agents are communicating, you can ask Claude to generate the payload schema — the actual JSON fields, data types, and one example value for each field. That schema goes on the diagram as a small annotation on the arrow between those two agents. It is a concrete signal that you have thought past the boxes and into the data contract. Here is the prompt to generate it.

shot.segment: Excellence move — data schema between agents
shot.command: My Agent 1 sends data to Agent 2. Agent 1's job: [JOB]. Agent 2's job: [JOB]. What does the message from Agent 1 to Agent 2 look like? Provide a simple JSON schema for the payload — just the fields that matter: the keys, their data types, and one example value for each.
output: JSON schema: key names · data types · one example value each
Put this schema as a small annotation on the arrow between the two agents

beats.narration_text: Four traps. Trap one: three agents that do the same thing. Naming them differently does not make them distinct. Each agent must have a different job and produce a different data output. If Agent 1 analyzes competitor data and Agent 2 also analyzes competitor data, you have one agent, not two. Trap two: no communication flow. A list of three agent descriptions is not an architecture — you need arrows showing what passes between the agents. If your diagram has boxes but no labeled arrows, the grader is looking at an org chart, not an architecture. Trap three: agents unrelated to the PRD problem. If your problem is campaign optimization, an agent that manages HR records is out of scope. Every agent must trace back to the problem you stated in Episode 3. Trap four: invented Madison features. If you name a component that does not exist in the Madison README, the instructor will notice. Stick to the real layers and real named components. When in doubt, verify in the README before putting it on the Figma board.

artifactLines: Trap 1 — Same job, different name: different name ≠ different agent; each must produce a different data output
Trap 2 — No communication flow: boxes without labeled arrows = org chart, not architecture
Trap 3 — Agents unrelated to the PRD problem: every agent must trace back to the problem in E3
Trap 4 — Invented Madison features: if the component is not in the Madison README, cut it
Fix: verify each component name in the README before committing to the Figma board
shot.sparkLine: distinct jobs → labeled arrows → PRD-relevant → README-verified

beats.narration_text: The rules for agent design. You need three agents, each with a different specific job and a different data output. Each agent must map to a real Madison layer — Intelligence, Content, Research, Experience, or Performance. You need a communication diagram with labeled arrows showing what data passes between the agents. The orchestration layer should be visible on the diagram as the coordination envelope. Excellence means putting a data schema — the actual JSON fields and types — on at least one arrow. AI drafted the suggestions; you own the truth. Before anything goes on the Figma board, verify every component name against the real Madison README.

artifactLines: 3 agents, each with a different specific job and a different data output
Each agent on a real Madison layer (Intelligence · Content · Research · Experience · Performance)
Communication diagram: labeled arrows showing what data passes between agents
Orchestration layer visible as the coordination envelope
Excellence: data schema (JSON fields + types) on at least one arrow
AI drafts the suggestions — you verify every component in the Madison README

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take your PRD problem statement from Episode 3 and paste it into this prompt. Claude will suggest three distinct agents and a starting communication flow. Then you do two things: first, refine each agent's job until it is specific enough that someone could actually build it — not 'analyze data' but 'pull weekly share-of-voice data from three named sources and rank them by brand.' Second, before you put any component name on the Figma board, open the Madison README from the assignment brief and verify it exists. That check takes thirty seconds and protects you from losing points on invented features. The diagram comes after the agents are right, not before.

shot.segment: Use your PRD problem from Episode 3
shot.command: My PRD problem: [PASTE YOUR PROBLEM STATEMENT]. The Madison layers available: Intelligence, Content, Research, Experience, Performance. Suggest 3 distinct agents for this project. For each: (1) a short agent name, (2) ONE specific job in one sentence — concrete enough that someone could build it, (3) which Madison layer it belongs to and which named component (if any) it uses, (4) what data it outputs. Make sure the three agents together address the full PRD problem without duplicating each other. Flag any agent whose named component is not verifiable in the Madison README. Then describe a simple communication flow: which agent sends what to which agent, in order.
shot.runningText: designing 3 Madison agents — verify components in the README before committing…
output: 3 agents: name · specific job · Madison layer · component · data output
Communication flow: agent → message → agent (in order)
Flags: any component not verifiable in the Madison README
